sentences.text: I gather that generally, everything deteriorated, because there were more and more people coming into the camp.
And it was just getting overcrowded.
And it tended to spread the illnesses, and the medical facilities just weren't up to it.
At that time, we hadn't got to the typhus yet.
But the typhus epidemic later on really killed many people in Belsen.
And again, I don't think our food rations were any less than we'd been getting before Kramer was there, though I wouldn't think that we actually noticed very much, in terms of Kramer arriving there.
But it was the general atmosphere and all these other people who were coming into the camp that made everything just generally worse.
I don't really know, in a way.
But it was just that people started to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shout, and-- and be ill, and eventually die.
No, not in our camp.
We were never beaten in our camp, [INAUDIBLE] So this is really where we were indeed privileged compared to what we've heard about other camps and maybe even other sections of Belsen.
But in our camp, I can't remember anybody being beaten or shot, anything like that.
No, we couldn't.
We were in our star camp.
We couldn't get out.
We never mingled with anybody outside the star camp.
There was barbed wire.
You will see, if you've seen the Anne Frank reconstruction recently, the latest thing, where somebody talked through the wire-- across the wire.
She said that we couldn't see through the wire, that there was cotton cloth or something like that in the wire, so you couldn't see the other camps.
I can't remember that we couldn't see the other camp.
But we certainly couldn't mix with them.
But you could talk, I suppose, across the wire to the other camps.
I can't remember that we ever did it.
But this woman claims she talked to Anne Frank from our camp to-- she was in our camp, and she talked to Anne Frank in the next camp.
But we weren't aware of any of these sort of things, no.
Did you meet up with your mother and Eve, at all?
Yes, we met every evening-- every evening.
The parents had to work in Belsen.
The kids did nothing all day.
We had our three periods of eating and queuing and Rudi with his food duties.
We might have done a bit here and there, sweeping up, or cleaning, or whatever, but otherwise we did nothing all day.
It was really boring.
And there was no opportunity to play football.
We had no footballs or balls, or anything like that.
So there may have been-- elderly people who did not have to work may have given us a certain amount of school, education instruction and religious instruction, maybe.
But again, I can't remember any of that, except as I told you.
When I came in, I knew nothing.
And when I came out, I knew quite a bit about Judaism.
So I picked it all up along with the languages and everything along the way.
Perhaps I should mention that earlier in 1944, in the summer of 1944, there was actually an exchange.
We were always waiting, of course, to be exchanged.
That was our hope and this is why we carried on, kind of thing.
And we could see that we were privileged.
And it was bad, but we were still alive.
And so in the summer of '44, there was an exchange to Palestine.
It's a long story, if you read the real thing, what happened.
But basically, around about 250 people who had certificates for Palestine, they were exchanged against Germans of a certain-- Templars, I think, or [DUTCH] in Dutch.
They were a German sect of monks or religious people that lived in Jerusalem.
And 250 of those were exchanged against 250 Jews from Belsen.
And amongst those were these people, Mainz, that lived across the road from us.
And after the war, we found out that all these people did actually get-- there was always a doubt that they would be just taken away somewhere else.
But they did actually go to Palestine.
Yes, we do remember their leaving, because at the last minute, somebody fell ill.
And they weren't allowed to go.
And they wanted replacements.
Rudi says they wanted a family of five, and that my father offered us straight away to go, but we were not accepted.
We were not sure now, because we didn't have the Palestine certificates, because I don't think we ever had them, although we'd applied for them.
And our British connections, apparently-- they didn't want-- they wanted people with Palestine certificates.
I don't think we had them.
So Rudi remembers that we tried.
And we nearly were on it, but we didn't.

So we do remember that particular episode, which to all those people, especially all those people with Palestine certificates-- because there were about a thousand, as we heard later-- it was a big disappointment, and to us, because at one time, we thought we might get on at the last minute.
Nevertheless, the fact that some people had apparently been exchanged-- and it was very high level people.
The high level people came-- we believe Himmler actually came to Belsen, and that he was there.

We saw some high level people in staff cars and big leather coats, and all that sort of thing-- this was before Kramer, in something like June '44-- came to supervise the exchange, and Red Cross people, and all that sort of thing.
So, yeah, it looked good.
And it obviously gave us hope that one day we might be exchanged.
And in fact, there was another exchange early in 1945 of mainly American people with American connections, we believe.
But we, again, were not called.
But it did give us hope in that we realized that there was still a possibility that we might get exchanged.
But early '45 things really got bad, because then the typhus fever started.
And people were dying.
In 1944, relatively few people, apparently, died in Belsen.
The numbers were not large in our camp of 4,000.
And I think altogether in Belsen, there was about 15,000 people there.
But in the early month of '45, it swelled to 60,000, or something like that, people, I understand.

And it really got very, very crowded in Belsen with all these people who were transferred from the East, who had been in Auschwitz and other camps, and who'd been on long train journeys, and lots of other camps, and on death marches, and all that.
And they all came to Belsen.
Then, you know, we got the lice.
So that was one of the pastimes that I remember very well, that we spent hours and hours every day hunting the lice.
We used to take all our clothes off and get them [INAUDIBLE].
They congregate in your hair.
We were shaven off by then kind of thing, but nevertheless, the lice got in your hair, and under your arms, and in the seams of your clothes.
And we turned all our clothes inside out.
And in the seams of the clothes were the lice.
And we'd pick them out and squash them to death kind of thing.
That was-- all day we spent doing that kind of thing, all of us-- great fun.
But we realized that it was the lice that was spreading the typhus epidemic.
And just-- they were just everywhere.
You'd kill them all one day, and they would be there next day.
The beds were infested with the lice.
And everybody had the lice everywhere.
No.
We-- we knew nothing about the gassing and all these other things, that I was aware of.

Obviously, some people must have heard the stories, because for example, this Dutch woman who claims to have spoken to Anne Frank and her sister across the wire, I think Anne Frank just said, when they asked about certain people, they just said they weren't there anymore.
Whether they said why or how, we don't know.
I mean, these are only reconstructions, anyway.
We can't remember having heard anything there about gas chambers and about everybody being killed, that sort of thing, no.
We-- wee couldn't meet with them.
We were still isolated in our particular section.
And there's actually an interesting story by this Regina Franks, who died, committed suicide last week.
She was an Auschwitz survivor, came to Belsen in January '45.
And when I told my story one day, she was in the audience.
And afterwards, she said she saw those people in that separate section of the camp with the yellow stars.
And she realized they were obviously Jews.
And she and all her mates from Auschwitz wondered who these people were, why they had their yellow stars and ordinary clothes.
And then one day, just before the end, they'd all been-- they'd all gone, which I'll come to later on.
And she never found out what happened to them.
She always thought that they might have all been shot.
She never knew what happened to them until I told my story, which you'll hear later on.
Then she found out that we were taken away and evacuated, actually.
She always wondered what happened to us, those people with yellow stars.
Anyway, so in early 1945, everybody started getting ill.
And people started to die.
It wasn't really clear whether people died of starvation, or exhaustion, or dysentery, or typhus, although typhus was slightly differently, really, with very high temperatures.
[INAUDIBLE] You probably could tell it, so maybe not so many of typhus.
And people from our camp-- I'm just talking about our camp in the star camp-- when people fell ill, they were taken to hospital.
We had a hospital barrack.
But there was no medicine.
There was no medical service at all.
So after a while, they died.
And the bodies would be taken out on a cart and taken just to the outside of our camp.
And there, other people would pick up the cart, and the bodies would be taken down to the bottom of the camp, where there was a crematorium.
And the bodies would be cremated.
That was the normal procedure.
But later, when too many people died, it all didn't work anymore, kind of thing.
So in the middle of January, my mother fell ill.
And she was taken to the hospital.
And we went to see her every evening.
And one evening, when we got there, she wasn't there anymore.
She'd died.
Even her body wasn't there, far as we know.
She died.
That was on the 17th of January, 1945.
We really didn't know exactly what she died of.
I mean, there were no death certificates, or anything like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was almost 43, a few days short of 43 years old.
This is the only time in the story where I get a bit upset and emotional, I'm sorry to say-- well, not sorry-- because we learned to live without-- without a mother.
It was particularly difficult for Eve, of course, because she was now alone in her barracks, seven years old.
And she was taken on by an orthodox couple called the Birnbaums.
He apparently was a teacher, middle aged, forties, or something like that.
And he had six children of their own.
They were very orthodox.
And they took on orphans and other people like Eve, who'd lost their mother.
And they were all alone, really, because we couldn't look after her.
Rudi, me, and my father were in another barrack.
So the Birnbaums took on Eve after my mother died.
And two months later, in March, the same thing happened to my father, really.
He was taken ill.
He couldn't go to work anymore, so he was taken to the hospital.
And we visited him in-- in the hospital.
And after a few days, he was gone.

This is where I have a very difficult situation with you and your colleagues at one time, about did you forget to say goodbye-- one of your sessions that you had in Montreal, I think, or something like that, with the therapists.
When I heard that, now, I-- I was quite moved by it.
But how could we, you see?
We visited my parents every evening.
And we couldn't just say, well, in case you die tomorrow, goodbye.
We just always said, see you tomorrow night.
And then they weren't there anymore, so we never did say goodbye.
There was always that hope they'd get better.
They'd lasted that long.

And especially my father, really, insofar as that he was-- I think it was the 20th of March, very late in March '45, only one month before the camp was liberated, before we were liberated, kind of thing.
And he'd survived in the camps for two years, Westerbork and Belsen.
To die just with one month to go-- it was very sad.
So now Rudi and me were also orphans.
Eve was a proper orphan.
And we continued to fend for ourselves, very like before, because we were in the camp all day.
My father was out all day anyway working, kind of thing.
So we carried on as before.
And everybody was dying, really, but-- in-- in the whole camp, not just in our star camp, but overall.
Apparently, 600 people were dying every day.
And the crematorium couldn't cope with it.
And we couldn't carry the bodies anymore.
People couldn't carry the bodies.
So this is when all the corpses were laid out just outside the barrack, basically.
And that's how these piles of skeletons developed.
All the clothes-- anything good that they had in the way of clothes-- everybody pinched, because it was winter.
Who pinched?
We all did.
Any clothes that we could find that were better than what we had, we would take.
I don't know whether the [GERMAN] got onto it, you know.
Many of those piles of bodies were inside our camp, kind of thing, so they wouldn't be able to get at those.
But once they got outside, and they probably had far more death than we had, you know, when we had 600 deaths a day, the vast majority of that was in the [GERMAN].

Relatively few of those were in our section of the camp, because the records show that out of the people that went from Westerbork to the star camp-- I think it was 4,000 people-- about half of them came back, survived.
Oh, yes, we kept on hoping.
We really-- we were boys.
It was almost-- I hate to say it-- an adventure story, really that we-- most of the other children, boys like us, they were surviving.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So I think that was sort of coming to the end of the Belsen story.
We could hear [INAUDIBLE] the planes more and more.
And in the end, we could actually hear the guns of the Allied forces in the distance-- artillery and all that.
We knew, of course, the Allies had landed a year before in 1944.
And we knew that.
And we knew the Allies were-- You knew that?
Yes, some of the underground people, like the barber and all that sort of thing, would find out all these kind of things.
And those-- we had nothing else to do all day except feed on scraps of rumors.
And anything-- any bits of information that anybody could glean from anywhere would go around the camp like wildfire.
So we knew that the Allies were winning and were on their way kind of thing.
And we could see it in the air.
And eventually, we could actually hear the artillery and the guns, which turned out to be the British army.
And it was at that time that we were first sent to-- all the people in the star camp were sent for a shower.
Again, we did have occasional showers, no gas chambers.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Incidentally, if I can just interrupt for one moment there, Judith, my daughter-- when I say I haven't been affected by the Holocaust at all, she says she has been affected by it.
She worries every time she goes under a shower.
I mean, I'm-- I'm terrified to hear that.
I never, ever thought about that, about being worried about going under a shower.
Anyway, by the by, we did have our showers in-- in Belsen.
And on the 9th of April, all the people that were left in the star camp were-- were walked back to the railway siding kind of thing.
And those people who couldn't walk, maybe like the orphanage people, Birnbaum and them, they were probably transported by truck, I think, to the railway siding.
And we were going to be evacuated.

I mean we were amazed-- kind of thing-- that the Germans should still persist in this idea of wanting to exchange us and keep us, and not wanting to fall us into Allied hands, because that's basically what it was.
So on the 9th of April, we went to the railway sidings and put into-- there were three trains there.
And we went into the third train.
And on the 10th of April, we left there.
Did you know where you were going to?
No, we didn't know where we were going to.

We I've heard since, and maybe at that time, there were rumors, that we were going to Theresienstadt, which was sort of supposed to be a model camp, a relatively good camp, by comparison, and sort of centrally situated between the Russians and the Americans, so one that would stay in German hands probably the longest.
So maybe we did know that we were aiming for that.
But we certainly didn't go in that direction, because we started off going north.
And Theresienstadt was going south, almost.
So we left Belsen on the 10th of April.
Who's we?
Rudi and me.
And we were all on this train, which turned out to be the third train, which turned out to have also the Birnbaums and the orphanage with all their people.
Basically, as far as we know, it was all the people from the star camp, about 2,000, 2,500 people. 2,500 people, maybe, that was left from the star camp all went onto this train.
We know that there were two other trains.

We believe one of them were a lot of Hungarians who were also destined for some kind of exchange, which Eichmann a long time previously tried to arrange-- Jews for trucks or Jews for money kind of thing.
And these Hungarians, about 2,000 of them, were in another section of Belsen not far from us.
And they apparently all went on one of these trains.
And then other people went on one of the other trains.
But we missed the best part of Belsen, really, which was the liberation, which happened on the 15th, five days after we left Belsen.

We weren't there for the liberation, which of course now is all the rage, the films, and [INAUDIBLE], and the speeches, and the horrible photographs, and films, and everything that everybody has seen about Belsen.
We missed it all.
We've heard all about it kind of thing.
But when we left on the 10th, we now hear, we now know that the British army was about 25 miles from Belsen.
And I've-- when I've given my talks, especially to Rotary Clubs, there's always somebody who was in the British army who was first into Belsen.
The whole British army has been to Belsen.
And they have, because the whole British army actually moved, swept through France, Belgium, Holland, Germany, and up toward Denmark.
They were not allowed to go to Berlin, because Berlin was supposed to be for the Russians.
There was some agreement.
So the whole British army moved up in that direction.
And they all came through Belsen.
And all of them claim to have seen Belsen.
And most of them have been there first.
Got a chap living just down the road.
He saw my article one day and rang me up.
He was one of the first in Belsen in the tanks.
But he didn't go in.
He got to Belsen, to the gates, and he saw what was inside.
And they carried on.
They were not allowed to stop, because it was a typhus area by that time.
And the British army was very hesitant about going inside.
But what happened at the liberation is fairly well known to many people.
So I won't dwell on that.
The train.
Yeah, the train was one of the best parts of our captivity, really, where Rudi came to the fore very much.
Basically, the train moved in small steps just to keep ahead of the Allied army.
So we never traveled very far, and not for very long.
We stood still for most of the time.
We-- I remember right at the beginning, we stopped in Soltau, which was the next big place.
And there, we met one of the other trains.
We think it was the one with the Hungarians.
And-- and we believe that in that station, we saw lots of foods.
And we all got out-- well, when we say lots of food, rotten turnips or something like that.
And then we all helped ourselves to these rotten turnips and things like that.
And there we also met other trains of evacuees, maybe non-Jews, and then we swapped and bartered anything that we had for food from these other trains.
But there was a lot of train activity in that area.
Later on, we never met any other trains.
We--
Pardon?
Yes, we were-- it appeared to be a mixed carriage train, because we definitely remember being in passenger coaches.
And there are other people who swear blind that there were cattle trucks.
And maybe they were both.
Why we know that we were in passenger coaches is that later in the story, we cooked food on the train.
And we used the seats, we used the planks from the seats to make fires.
We broke them up, and we used those planks to make fires on the-- on the-- at the end of each passenger coach, there was a sort of passage that goes across the train.
And on that bit, in the open air, we lit fires, and we cooked.
Rudi was the main one, of course.
We cooked the raw potatoes, and the grass, and anything else that we could find in the fields.
And we cooked it there.
So we definitely know we were on a passenger coach.
But it might have been a mixed train.
There were also-- there were SS guards on the train.
But they didn't really bother us too much, because they were-- they could see that it was getting to the end.
And they were more worried about their own future, I think, than they were worried about us.
But there was an anti-aircraft gun, or some kind of military equipment at the back of our train.
So despite the fact that we put white flags out, and we put big white sheets on top of the train, the train was often attacked by Allied planes.
And I particularly remember one day that we were attacked by fighter planes with twin fuselage-- very distinctive type of fighter planes.
And I found out after the war that these were P-38 Lightnings-- American planes.
And they attacked our plane.
And we would all get out of the train.
We would all lie in the fields, and we would watch.
It was great fun, in a way, to see these planes diving down and shooting at the train and starting the odd fire.
But they never actually disabled the train entirely, so we always managed to move on.

But this business about getting out of the train, which the SS didn't stop us, started us-- whilst we were lying in the fields, we were taking the grass and cabbage, or any kinds of greens that we could see, and the potatoes.
We would dig them out of the field, and we'd take them back to the train.
And then we would cook them and eat them, because they left us no food.
We had no food or water or toilet facilities or anything on the train.
But we stopped a lot, and that's when we did everything-- in the fields.
And because the train stopped so often, we could just go further and further away.
The engine driver would whistle before he'd go off again kind of thing, and then we could all get back on the train again.
And it was sort of like a holiday outing, in a way, come to think of it.
We'd go further and further afield, and we'd get into nearby villages.
And there, we would get food off the Germans, either by begging or by stealing or by looting kind of thing.
And Rudi was to the fore in those sort of activities.
No, we didn't.
As far as I know, we didn't speak to them, because we tended to speak Dutch all the time-- all the time between us, not really German, so no.
We-- we had no relationships with them at all.
We were just trying to get food off them one way or the other, kind of thing, without anything else.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
It's interesting you should say that, because one day when I was giving this talk, again-- to Rotary Club, lots of military people-- somebody at the end said, why did you go back to the train?
And I was totally flabbergasted by that question.
It was something that we never, ever really thought about.
Now, when we see all these films about [INAUDIBLE] and the Wooden Horse, and all these other escape films, we're wondering what we missed, and why we never tried anything.
But our excuse is that all military prisoners of war, their first duty, apparently, was to escape or to try to escape.
We never had anything.
Our parents had never, ever tried to escape.
And they never, ever told us that the objective was to escape, or we should try and escape.
We were always indoctrinated with this idea that we should stick to the rules.
And we would play the game as it was laid down.
So we could have easily escaped in the fields and when we went to nearby villages.
All we had to do was not go back to the train.
But as far as we know, nobody did it.
I think in some ways, we were really a community.
We'd lived together in the star camp for two or three years now.
And we belonged together.
And we were going to see it out together.
And we were still hoping for the best.

I think if we had stayed in the village or in the fields, if somebody had seen us with our shaven heads and obviously, the star-- we could have taken the stars off, and all that sort of thing-- but we didn't look like Germans, really.
We were skeletons.
And the Germans would have alerted the SS or the Gestapo.
And we'd have been shot.
So I don't think we'd have done very well.
Even though we could speak-- most of them couldn't speak German.
We could speak a bit of German.
But I don't think we'd have got away with it.
But it was a very interesting question, why we never tried to escape.
